statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall averages remaining near 90-day thresholds.
fullAnalysis:
The EU's fuel security position shows significant stress across member states, with 19 of 27 countries reporting critical stock levels in at least one fuel category. While EU-wide averages remain close to the 90-day mandatory minimum (petrol 98.9 days, diesel 97.8 days, jet fuel 91.9 days), the distribution reveals concerning vulnerabilities. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status, including petrol at just 33.2 days and diesel at 32.4 days.

Jet fuel stocks present the most widespread concern, with 17 of 27 countries below the 90-day threshold. This includes major economies like Germany (61.4 days), France (59.6 days), and the Netherlands (59.0 days). The aviation fuel shortage reflects both increased demand recovery and supply chain pressures from Middle East disruptions that have contributed to strategic reserve releases.

Oil prices have surged 40.2% since late February, reaching $101.63 per barrel, directly impacting fuel costs across the bloc. The Netherlands leads with the highest petrol prices at €2.279 per liter, while significant price disparities exist with Malta offering the cheapest at €1.34 per liter. This price volatility, combined with depleted reserves, suggests continued pressure on EU energy security in the near term.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries report critical fuel stock levels in at least one category, indicating widespread supply pressure
Jet fuel reserves are most affected, with 17 countries below the 90-day threshold, including major economies Germany, France, and Netherlands
Romania faces the most severe shortages across all fuel types, with petrol at 33.2 days and diesel at 32.4 days - both critically low
Oil prices have surged 40.2% since late February to $101.63/barrel following Middle East supply disruptions
Fuel price disparities remain significant, with Netherlands petrol costing 70% more than Malta despite both being EU members
